source.title: Cumulative use of strong anticholinergics and incident dementia: a prospective cohort study

confidence_rationale: Strong prospective design with objective pharmacy dispensing data and 10-year cumulative exposure window; clear dose-response curve (HR 1.54 at highest dose tier). High source quality. Mapping fidelity is moderate: the association is well-documented but the mechanism is undetermined -- the paper cannot distinguish the nocturnal REM pathway from the diurnal cholinergic deficit pathway (both predicted by MECH-175). Transfer risk is low: this is exactly the population (65+, community-dwelling, followed prospectively) that MECH-175 addresses.
failure_signatures: The study cannot distinguish REM suppression from diurnal cholinergic deficit as the mediating mechanism -- both are MECH-175 pathways but the epidemiology alone cannot separate them.

Reverse causation cannot be fully excluded: prodromal dementia may increase anticholinergic prescribing (though authors excluded the most recent 12 months of use and show association for exposure 1-11 years prior).

Most anticholinergic classes used were tricyclic antidepressants, first-generation antihistamines, and bladder antimuscarinics -- REM suppression potency varies substantially across these classes, which is not captured by the TSDD metric.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Higher cumulative anticholinergic use (total standardized daily doses over 10 years) is associated with increased risk of dementia and Alzheimer's disease in a dose-response manner; HR 1.54 (95% CI 1.21-1.96) for >1095 TSDDs vs no use.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-175 predicts this association via two independent pathways: (1) cumulative REM suppression degrading MECH-123 precision recalibration nightly, and (2) cumulative diurnal cholinergic deficit mimicking the basal forebrain cholinergic loss of Alzheimer's disease. The dose-response relationship is consistent with pathway (1) or (2) or both operating cumulatively.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The TSDD metric does not stratify by REM suppression potency -- diphenhydramine (high ACh, high REM suppression) and oxybutynin (high ACh, primarily peripheral) are given equal weight. A mechanism-faithful analysis would weight by REM suppression power, not just anticholinergic burden. The paper provides epidemiological support for MECH-175 but cannot test the dual-pathway decomposition (see Q-031).
mapping.source_context: Prospective cohort, community-dwelling adults 65+, Group Health (Seattle), n=3434, mean follow-up 7.3 years; computerised pharmacy dispensing data; dementia via standard diagnostic criteria.

confidence_components.notes: Weighted toward source_quality given strong epidemiological design. Mapping fidelity limited by mechanism-agnostic TSDD metric.
